lessons.title: Week 1: Course Overview and Review of Coordinate Geometry
learning_objectives: Review rectangular and polar coordinate relationships used throughout route surveying.
Compute inverse (distance and direction) and forward (coordinate) problems from station data.
Apply the coordinate (DMD/shoelace) method for area as the foundation for earthwork and boundary work.
key_takeaways: Coordinate geometry (COGO) reduces every surveying computation to forward and inverse operations on (N,E) points.
The inverse uses hypot for distance and atan2 for azimuth; the forward uses sin and cos of the azimuth.
Area by coordinates is exact for any closed polygon and underlies cross-section and parcel computations.

content.html:
Why coordinate geometry drives advanced surveying

SRVY 2341 builds on the traverse, leveling, and measurement skills you developed earlier, but nearly every advanced computation — a curve PI, an earthwork cross-section, a state plane reduction — ultimately reduces to operations on rectangular coordinates (N, E). This course assumes you can move fluently between polar data (a distance and a direction) and rectangular data (a northing and an easting). The two recurring operations are the forward computation (given a point, a direction, and a distance, find the next point) and the inverse computation (given two points, find the distance and direction between them).

In Texas practice, directions are usually carried as azimuths from north or as quadrant bearings. Internally, software and your calculator work cleanest in azimuths because the trigonometry is unambiguous over a full 360 degrees.

content.latex: L = \sqrt{\Delta N^2 + \Delta E^2}, \quad Az = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta E, \Delta N)
content.label: Inverse computation

content.html:
Area by coordinates

The coordinate method (equivalently the shoelace or DMD method) gives the exact area of any closed, non-self-intersecting polygon directly from its corner coordinates. It is the engine behind parcel acreage, earthwork end areas, and pond volumes. Order the vertices consistently (clockwise or counter-clockwise) and the magnitude of the result is the area regardless of direction; the sign only tells you the traverse sense.

  • Always close the polygon back to the first point.
  • Use full-precision coordinates — rounding intermediate values corrupts the doubled sum.
  • Divide acres by 43,560 sq ft only at the very end.

content.text: Texas differs: many Texas records carry directions as quadrant bearings tied to a specific basis (e.g., GLO, prior deed call, or a grid bearing on a named SPCS zone). Always state your basis of bearing on the plat; mixing a grid azimuth with a geodetic or astronomic bearing is a common and serious error.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Forward and inverse computations are the two operations behind all COGO.
Carry directions as azimuths internally; convert to bearings for the deed.
Area by coordinates is exact and is reused for cross-sections and parcels.

flashcards.definition: Given a starting coordinate, an azimuth, and a distance, compute the coordinate of the next point: N2 = N1 + L cos Az, E2 = E1 + L sin Az.
Given two coordinates, compute the distance (hypot of the differences) and the azimuth (atan2 of departure over latitude) between them.
A method that computes the exact area of a closed polygon from its vertex coordinates as half the absolute value of the cross-product sum.

lessons.title: Week 2: Simple Horizontal Curves — Terminology and Geometry
learning_objectives: Define the geometric elements of a simple horizontal curve (PC, PI, PT, R, I, T, L, E, M, LC).
Compute the tangent, length, external, and middle ordinate from R and I.
Relate degree of curve (arc and chord definition) to radius.
key_takeaways: A simple curve is a single circular arc joining two tangents that intersect at the PI with deflection angle I.
Tangent T = R tan(I/2) and arc length L = R I (with I in radians) are the two master equations.
Degree of curve (arc definition) is the central angle subtending a 100-ft arc: D = 5729.58/R.

content.html:
The simple horizontal curve

A simple curve is a single circular arc of radius R that smoothly connects two straight tangent lines. The tangents, if extended, meet at the point of intersection (PI), and the angle between them is the intersection (deflection) angle I — equal to the central angle of the arc. The curve begins at the point of curvature (PC), where it leaves the back tangent, and ends at the point of tangency (PT), where it rejoins the forward tangent. Because the radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the PC and PT, the geometry is fully determined by just two quantities: R and I.

Everything else — tangent distance, curve length, external distance, middle ordinate, and long chord — follows from R and I by simple right-triangle trigonometry on the half-angle I/2.

content.alt: Simple horizontal curve showing PC, PI, PT, R, I, T, L, E, M
content.caption: Figure: Geometry of a simple circular curve and its elements.

content.latex: T = R\tan\!\left(\tfrac{I}{2}\right), \quad L = \frac{\pi R I^{\circ}}{180} = R\,I_{\text{rad}}
content.label: Tangent and arc length

content.html:
Degree of curve

U.S. route surveying often specifies curve sharpness by degree of curve, D, rather than radius. Under the arc definition (standard for highways), D is the central angle that subtends a 100-ft arc; under the chord definition (traditional railroad practice), D subtends a 100-ft chord. A larger D means a sharper, smaller-radius curve. Arc and chord definitions agree to within a few hundredths of a foot for flat curves but diverge for sharp ones, so always state which definition governs.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A simple curve is fully defined by R and I.
Master equations: T = R tan(I/2), L = R*I (I in radians).
Degree of curve (arc) D = 5729.58/R; larger D = sharper curve.

flashcards.definition: The point where the two tangents of a curve, extended, intersect. The angle there is the intersection angle I, equal to the curve's central angle.
The distance from the PC (or PT) to the PI, equal to R tan(I/2).
The central angle subtending a 100-ft arc, D = 5729.58/R. Larger degree means a sharper curve.

lessons.title: Week 3: Simple Horizontal Curves — Stakeout and Layout
learning_objectives: Compute PC and PT stations from the PI station, T, and L.
Lay out a simple curve by the deflection-angle (chord) method.
Compute deflection angles and chord lengths to even stations on the curve.
key_takeaways: PC station = PI station - T; PT station = PC station + L (never PI + T).
The deflection angle from the back tangent to any curve point is half the subtended arc's central angle.
Field layout proceeds by occupying the PC and turning deflection angles while taping (or EDM) chord distances.

content.html:
From geometry to the ground

Designing a curve gives you R, I, T, and L; staking it requires putting points on the ground at regular stations along the arc. The first step is stationing. The PI station is known from the alignment. Because the curve departs the back tangent at the PC, you subtract the tangent distance from the PI station to get the PC, then add the arc length (not the tangent) to reach the PT.

  • PC station = PI station − T
  • PT station = PC station + L

A classic blunder is computing the PT as PI + T; the curve is shorter than going out to the PI and back, so this is always wrong.

Deflection-angle (chord) method

The traditional and still common field method stakes the curve from the PC by turning deflection angles from the back tangent and measuring chords to each point. The governing principle is the inscribed angle theorem: the deflection angle from the tangent to any point on the curve equals half the central angle subtended by the arc from the PC to that point. So the total deflection from PC to PT is exactly I/2.

content.label: Deflection angle and chord to a point at arc distance L from PC

content.text: Modern practice stakes curves by coordinates with a total station or RTK GPS — compute (N,E) of each curve point and stake by radial or RTK methods. The deflection-angle method remains the conceptual check and the exam standard, and it is the fallback when only a total station on the PC is available.

content.alt: Deflection-angle layout from PC with cumulative deflections to even stations and PT
content.caption: Figure: Staking a simple curve by deflection angles and chords from the PC.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: PC = PI station − T; PT = PC + L (use arc length, not tangent).
Deflection to any point = half its subtended central angle; total = I/2.
Coordinate staking has largely replaced chords, but deflections remain the check.

flashcards.definition: PC = PI − T and PT = PC + L. The PT is found by adding arc length to the PC, never by adding T to the PI.
The angle turned at the PC from the back tangent to a point on the curve; equal to half the central angle subtending the arc to that point. Total deflection PC to PT is I/2.
A partial chord shorter than the standard 100-ft chord, used at the start (PC to first even station) and end (last even station to PT) of a curve.

lessons.title: Week 4: Compound and Reverse Curves
learning_objectives: Distinguish compound, reverse, and spiral curves and state where each is appropriate.
Solve a two-arc compound curve given the common tangent and component radii/angles.
Explain spiral transition curves, superelevation, and why reverse curves need separation.
key_takeaways: A compound curve is two same-direction arcs of different radii meeting at a PCC on a common tangent.
A reverse curve is two opposite-direction arcs meeting at a PRC; abrupt reversals require a spiral or tangent between them.
Spirals provide a gradual change in curvature so superelevation can be introduced safely at design speed.

content.html:
Beyond the simple curve

Terrain and design constraints often prevent a single radius from fitting an alignment. Two extensions are common. A compound curve consists of two (or more) circular arcs curving in the same direction but with different radii, joined at a point of compound curvature (PCC) where they share a common tangent. A reverse curve consists of two arcs curving in opposite directions, joined at a point of reverse curvature (PRC).

Compound curves let an alignment hug variable terrain (e.g., interchange ramps). Reverse curves are useful for offset alignments but are hazardous at speed because a vehicle's steering and superelevation must reverse instantly at the PRC.

content.latex: T_{\text{total}} \text{ found from } \; T_1=R_1\tan\tfrac{I_1}{2},\; T_2=R_2\tan\tfrac{I_2}{2},\; I=I_1+I_2
content.label: Compound-curve tangent components

content.html:
Spiral transition curves and superelevation

On high-speed routes, jumping abruptly from a straight tangent (infinite radius) to a circular arc (finite radius R) would require an instantaneous lateral acceleration. A spiral (clothoid) inserts a transition along which the radius decreases uniformly from infinity to R, so curvature — and the lateral force a driver feels — builds gradually. The spiral also provides the length over which superelevation (the banking, e) is rotated in, counteracting centrifugal force. AASHTO design relates the superelevation rate e and side-friction factor f to design speed V and radius R.

content.label: Minimum radius for design speed (V in mph, e and f as decimals)

content.text: Reverse curves without an intervening tangent or spiral are discouraged for highways because superelevation cannot reverse instantaneously. Provide a tangent or compound spiral between opposing arcs so the cross-slope can be transitioned through level.

content.alt: Compound curve (PCC) and reverse curve (PRC) compared on a plan view
content.caption: Figure: Compound vs. reverse curve geometry and junction points.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Compound = same-direction arcs at a PCC; reverse = opposite-direction arcs at a PRC.
Spirals make curvature change gradual and host superelevation runoff.
Reverse curves need separation (tangent/spiral) so banking can transition safely.

flashcards.definition: The common point where two same-direction circular arcs of different radii meet on a shared tangent in a compound curve.
The point where two opposite-direction arcs meet in a reverse curve; the radius changes sense here.
A clothoid whose radius decreases uniformly from infinity to R, providing gradual curvature change and the length over which superelevation is introduced.

lessons.title: Week 5: Vertical Curves — Theory and Computation
learning_objectives: Describe a parabolic vertical curve and define crest vs. sag curves.
Compute elevations along an equal-tangent vertical curve from the BVC.
Locate the turning point (high or low point) of a vertical curve.
key_takeaways: Vertical curves are parabolas connecting two grades; the rate of grade change r = (g2 - g1)/L is constant.
Elevation = BVC elevation + g1*x + (r/2)*x^2, with x measured in stations from the BVC.
The turning point is where the tangent grade reaches zero: x = -g1/r stations from the BVC.

content.html:
Vertical curves: parabolas, not arcs

Where two roadway grades (g1 in, g2 out, expressed as percent or as a decimal rise/run) meet at a point of vertical intersection (PVI), a smooth vertical curve joins them. Unlike horizontal curves, vertical curves are equal-tangent parabolas, not circular arcs, because a parabola gives a constant rate of grade change and a comfortable, constant vertical acceleration. The curve begins at the BVC (begin vertical curve, also PVC) and ends at the EVC (end vertical curve, also PVT), each L/2 from the PVI.

A crest curve occurs when the grade decreases (g1 > g2), forming a hill; a sag curve occurs when the grade increases (g1 < g2), forming a valley. Crest curves are usually governed by stopping sight distance; sag curves by headlight sight distance and rider comfort.

content.latex: r = \frac{g_2 - g_1}{L}\quad\text{(per station)}, \qquad Y = Y_{BVC} + g_1 x + \tfrac{r}{2}x^2
content.label: Parabolic elevation (x in stations from BVC)

content.html:
The turning point

The high point of a crest curve or the low point of a sag curve — the turning point — is where the instantaneous grade equals zero. Because the grade along the parabola is g1 + r*x, setting it to zero gives x_t = -g1/r stations measured from the BVC. The turning point matters for drainage (the low point of a sag fixes where inlets go) and for sight-distance checks at crests. If x_t falls outside the range 0 to L, the curve has no interior turning point and the controlling elevation is at an endpoint.

content.text: Keep grades in percent and lengths in stations (1 station = 100 ft) so r comes out in percent-per-station. Mixing feet and stations is the most common vertical-curve blunder.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Vertical curves are equal-tangent parabolas with constant rate r = (g2-g1)/L.
Elevation: Y = Y_BVC + g1*x + (r/2)x^2, x in stations from BVC.
Turning point at x = -g1/r; crest if g1>g2, sag if g1<g2.

flashcards.definition: An equal-tangent parabola connecting two roadway grades meeting at the PVI, giving a constant rate of grade change.
A crest curve forms a hill (grade decreases, g1>g2); a sag curve forms a valley (grade increases, g1<g2).
The high or low point of a vertical curve, where the grade is zero, located x = -g1/r stations from the BVC.

lessons.title: Week 6: Vertical Curves — Design and Stakeout
learning_objectives: Apply sight-distance criteria (K value) to select vertical curve length.
Compute finished-grade elevations at even stations for staking.
Stake a vertical curve and check clearance and drainage at the turning point.
key_takeaways: The K value (L per percent of grade change, K = L/A) packages sight-distance design into one number.
Curve length L = K*A, where A = |g2 - g1| is the algebraic grade change in percent.
Staking a vertical curve means tabulating finished elevations at each even station for grade stakes.

content.html:
Designing the length: the K value

Choosing how long a vertical curve should be is a sight-distance problem. AASHTO consolidates the geometry into a single design parameter, the K value, defined as the curve length per one percent of total grade change: K = L / A, where A = |g2 − g1| is the algebraic difference of grades in percent. Each design speed has a tabulated minimum K for crest curves (stopping sight distance) and for sag curves (headlight beam distance). To design, look up K for the speed and curve type, then compute L = K·A and round up to a convenient length.

content.text: K values above are representative AASHTO stopping-sight-distance figures for illustration; always design from the current AASHTO Green Book and the governing agency (TxDOT) tables, which control on Texas projects.

content.html:
Staking the finished grade

Field staking of a vertical curve produces grade stakes: at each even station the surveyor computes the finished-grade elevation Y = Y_BVC + g1*x + (r/2)x^2 and marks cut or fill relative to existing ground. The surveyor tabulates station, x (stations from BVC), tangent elevation, parabolic offset, and finished elevation. At the turning point the crew verifies drainage (sag) or sight clearance (crest). A useful check: the vertical offset from the tangent at the PVI equals A*L/8 (in feet when A is in percent and L in stations).

content.label: Mid-curve offset from tangent at the PVI

content.alt: Vertical curve staking table and profile with cuts/fills at even stations
content.caption: Figure: Finished-grade elevations and the mid-curve tangent offset.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: K = L/A turns sight-distance design into one lookup; L = K*A.
A = |g2 - g1| is the algebraic grade change in percent.
Stake finished elevations at even stations; mid-curve offset = A*L/8.

flashcards.definition: The vertical curve length per one percent of grade change, K = L/A. Minimum K values come from sight-distance design tables by speed and curve type.
The absolute difference between the two grades, A = |g2 - g1|, in percent; controls curve length and the mid-curve offset.
The vertical distance from the grade tangent to the curve at the PVI, equal to A*L/8 (feet, with A in percent and L in stations).

lessons.title: Week 7: Route Surveying and Alignment Design
learning_objectives: Outline the phases of route surveying from reconnaissance to final alignment.
Combine horizontal and vertical alignment into a 3D design that meets sight and grade limits.
Explain stationing, equations, and the role of the centerline in route projects.
key_takeaways: Route surveying proceeds reconnaissance to preliminary to location to construction, refining the centerline at each phase.
The horizontal alignment (tangents + curves) and vertical alignment (grades + vertical curves) must be coordinated in 3D.
Station equations reconcile changes in alignment length so continuous stationing is preserved.

content.html:
The route surveying process

Route surveying is the body of work that locates and stakes a linear project — a highway, pipeline, transmission line, or rail line. It follows recognized phases: reconnaissance (broad corridor study, often from maps, LiDAR, and aerial imagery), preliminary survey (topographic mapping of candidate corridors), location survey (fixing the final centerline on the ground), and construction survey (staking the design for the contractor). At each phase the centerline — the master reference line of tangents and curves — is refined and its stationing carried forward.

Coordinating horizontal and vertical alignment

A route is a three-dimensional object: the horizontal alignment (tangents joined by horizontal curves) and the vertical alignment (grades joined by vertical curves) must be designed together. Good practice keeps the two in phase — for example, placing vertical curve crests and sags so they do not hide a horizontal curve, and avoiding a sharp horizontal curve at the bottom of a steep grade. Sight distance, drainage, and superelevation transitions all couple the two alignments.

Stationing and station equations

Distance along the centerline is measured by stationing (e.g., 12+50 means 1,250 ft from the project origin). When an alignment is revised and the new length differs from the old, surveyors insert a station equation at the junction (for example, "Sta 45+20 back = Sta 45+00 ahead") so that downstream stationing is preserved without renumbering the whole project. Station equations are documented on the plans and must be honored in all staking.

content.text: On Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT) projects, alignments, stationing, and superelevation are delivered in standardized digital formats; the field surveyor should verify the control datum, the SPCS zone, and any combined scale factor before staking from design coordinates.

content.alt: Plan-and-profile sheet showing coordinated horizontal and vertical alignment with stationing
content.caption: Figure: Coordinated horizontal/vertical alignment on a plan-and-profile sheet.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Route work flows reconnaissance to preliminary to location to construction.
Horizontal and vertical alignments are designed together in 3D.
Station equations reconcile length changes and keep stationing continuous.

flashcards.definition: The surveying of a linear project (road, pipeline, rail) through reconnaissance, preliminary, location, and construction phases, centered on refining the centerline.
A documented statement (e.g., 45+20 back = 45+00 ahead) reconciling a change in alignment length so downstream stationing stays continuous.
The master reference line of an alignment, composed of tangents and curves, along which stationing is measured and from which staking offsets are taken.

lessons.title: Week 8: Midterm Exam
learning_objectives: Review and self-assess horizontal and vertical curve computations covered in Weeks 1-7.
Demonstrate mastery of curve stationing, deflection layout, and parabolic elevation.
Identify weak areas to target before the second half of the course.
key_takeaways: The midterm covers coordinate geometry, simple/compound/reverse curves, and vertical curves.
Bring full-precision through every multi-step curve computation; round only the final answer.
Check answers with independent relationships (e.g., total deflection = I/2; PT check).

content.html:
Midterm exam — scope and strategy

The Week 8 midterm assesses the first half of SRVY 2341: coordinate geometry (forward/inverse, area), the geometry and stakeout of simple horizontal curves, compound and reverse curves, and the theory and computation of vertical curves. Expect a mix of conceptual multiple-choice questions and multi-step numeric problems. Allocate time so that a single hard problem does not consume the period.

The most reliable way to lose points is rounding too early. Carry coordinates and angles at full precision and round only the reported answer to the requested units. Wherever a problem offers an independent check, use it.

rows: Find T, L, E, M, LC from R and I

content.text: Useful self-checks: total curve deflection from PC to PT must equal I/2; PT station must equal PC + L; a vertical-curve elevation at the PVI station equals the tangent elevation plus the A*L/8 offset.

content.html:
Common pitfalls to avoid

Watch the usual traps: computing the PT as PI + T instead of PC + L; mixing grid azimuths with quadrant bearings without converting; using I in degrees where the arc-length formula needs radians; and keeping vertical-curve grades in percent while measuring x in feet instead of stations. A quick units pass before submitting catches most of these.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Midterm spans Weeks 1-7: COGO, horizontal curves, vertical curves.
Carry full precision; round only final answers.
Use built-in checks: deflection = I/2, PT = PC + L.

flashcards.definition: Coordinate geometry, simple horizontal curves and stakeout, compound/reverse curves, and vertical curves (Weeks 1-7).
The point of tangency station must equal PC + L (arc length), and the total deflection PC to PT must equal I/2 — two independent checks on a curve solution.

lessons.title: Week 9: Earthwork Volumes — Cross Sections and Computation
learning_objectives: Compute cross-section end areas by the coordinate method.
Apply the average-end-area and prismoidal formulas to find earthwork volumes.
Distinguish cut, fill, and the role of shrinkage/swell and mass haul.
key_takeaways: End area is the cross-sectional area of cut or fill at a station, found by the coordinate method.
Volume by average end area = (A1 + A2)/2 * distance; the prismoidal formula adds a middle area for accuracy.
A mass-haul diagram cumulates net earthwork to plan economical haul and balance points.

content.html:
From cross-sections to volumes

Earthwork quantities drive the cost of route projects. At each station the surveyor (or the DTM software) builds a cross-section: the existing ground profile overlaid on the design template, with the area of cut above grade and fill below grade. Each of these end areas is computed by the same coordinate (shoelace) method used for parcels, applied to the polygon between ground and template.

Volume between two adjacent cross-sections is then obtained by multiplying a representative area by the distance between them.

content.label: Prismoidal formula (A_m = area of the middle section)

content.html:
Shrinkage, swell, and mass haul

Computed cut and fill are in-place (bank) volumes. Excavated material loses volume when compacted into fill (shrinkage) or gains volume when loosened (swell); both are accounted for by a factor applied to cut. To plan the economical movement of earth, surveyors build a mass-haul diagram that plots cumulative net earthwork (cut positive, fill negative) versus station. Rising segments indicate net cut, falling segments net fill; horizontal balance lines identify reaches where cut and fill offset, and the area between the curve and a balance line measures haul (volume times distance).

content.text: Convert cubic feet to cubic yards by dividing by 27 only at the end. End areas in square feet times station distances in feet give cubic feet; the bid item is almost always cubic yards.

content.alt: Mass-haul diagram with balance lines, cut/fill reaches, and haul areas
content.caption: Figure: Mass-haul diagram showing balance points and haul.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: End areas come from the coordinate method on the ground-vs-template polygon.
Volume by average end area = (A1+A2)/2 * d; prismoidal is more accurate.
Mass-haul diagrams plan economical earth movement and balance points.

flashcards.definition: The cross-sectional area of cut or fill at a single station, computed by the coordinate method from the ground and template points.
Volume = (A1 + A2)/2 times the distance between sections; the standard method for routine earthwork.
A plot of cumulative net earthwork versus station used to plan economical haul, identify balance points, and measure haul volume-distance.

lessons.title: Week 10: Construction Staking Procedures
learning_objectives: Describe offset, slope, and grade staking for construction.
Compute slope-stake catch points from template, slope ratio, and ground.
Explain blue tops, cut/fill marks, and stake marking conventions.
key_takeaways: Construction staking transfers the design to the ground with offset, slope, and grade stakes.
A slope stake marks the catch point where the design side slope meets existing ground.
Blue tops set finished grade; cut/fill is marked relative to a reference offset stake.

content.html:
Staking the design for the contractor

Construction staking converts plan-and-profile design into marks the contractor builds to. Because stakes at the exact design location would be destroyed by equipment, surveyors set offset stakes a known distance to the side and write the cut or fill and the offset on the stake. The crew reads the stake, applies the offset, and grades to the design.

Three staking products recur on route projects: slope stakes (where side slopes meet ground), blue tops (finished-grade hubs), and grade/offset stakes for structures and pipe.

Slope staking and catch points

A slope stake locates the catch point (daylight point) where a designed side slope intersects existing ground. Its offset from centerline is not fixed in advance; it depends on the slope ratio s:1 (horizontal:vertical), the depth of cut or fill, and the ground slope, so it is found by trial or by a direct formula. For level ground at a cut of depth h with side slope s:1 and half-width (template) w, the catch offset from centerline is w + s*h.

content.label: Catch-point offset (level ground; w = template half-width, s = slope ratio, h = cut/fill depth)

content.text: Stake marking conventions: 'C' denotes cut (excavate to reach grade), 'F' denotes fill (add material). Always state the offset and the reference (centerline, EP, etc.). Field books and stake faces should be unambiguous to the equipment operator.

content.alt: Slope stake at catch point with template half-width, side slope, and cut depth
content.caption: Figure: Slope-stake geometry and the catch point on a cut section.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Offset stakes keep references clear of the work; cut/fill and offset are written on them.
Slope stakes mark the catch point where side slope meets ground.
Catch offset on level ground = template half-width + slope ratio * depth.

flashcards.definition: A stake marking the catch point where a designed side slope daylights into existing ground; its offset depends on slope ratio, depth, and ground slope.
The point where the design side slope meets existing ground; on level ground its offset from centerline is w + s*h.
A hub driven and marked to the exact finished subgrade elevation, used by the contractor to fine-grade.

lessons.title: Week 11: Topographic Surveying Methods
learning_objectives: Compare topographic data-collection methods: total station, RTK GPS, and LiDAR.
Explain DTM/TIN surfaces and how contours are interpolated.
Apply contour rules and select an appropriate contour interval.
key_takeaways: Topographic surveys capture 3D ground points that become a DTM/TIN surface for mapping and design.
Contours are interpolated from the surface; they never cross and close on themselves or the map edge.
Contour interval is chosen for terrain relief and map purpose; closer spacing means steeper ground.

content.html:
Capturing the ground surface

A topographic survey measures enough 3D points to model the ground and its features. Methods range from total station radial shots (selective, high accuracy), to RTK GPS (fast, open-sky), to airborne and mobile LiDAR and photogrammetry (massive point density over large areas). The collected points feed a digital terrain model (DTM), commonly stored as a triangulated irregular network (TIN) that connects points into triangular facets, with breaklines enforcing ridges, ditches, and pavement edges.

From surface to contours

Contours are lines of equal elevation interpolated across the TIN. Reading and drawing them correctly requires a few rules: contours never cross or split (except at a vertical cliff); they close on themselves or run off the map edge; evenly spaced contours indicate uniform slope, while closely spaced contours indicate steep ground; contours form V-shapes pointing upstream when they cross a stream and point downhill around a ridge. The contour interval — the elevation difference between adjacent contours — is chosen from the terrain relief and the map's purpose.

content.text: Choose a small contour interval (e.g., 1 ft) for flat, detailed sites and a larger interval (5-20 ft) for rugged terrain or small-scale mapping. The interval must suit both the relief and the accuracy of the source data.

content.alt: TIN surface with breaklines and interpolated contours over varied terrain
content.caption: Figure: TIN surface and contours, including V-rules at a stream and ridge.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Topo methods (total station, RTK, LiDAR) build a DTM/TIN with breaklines.
Contours never cross, close on themselves or the edge, and bunch on steep ground.
Pick the contour interval for terrain relief and map purpose.

flashcards.definition: A 3D model of the ground surface, often stored as a TIN of triangular facets with breaklines, built from surveyed points.
The constant elevation difference between adjacent contour lines; smaller for flat detailed sites, larger for rugged terrain.
A linear feature (ridge, ditch, pavement edge) that constrains TIN triangulation so the surface honors abrupt grade changes.

lessons.title: Week 12: State Plane Coordinate Systems and Map Projections
learning_objectives: Explain the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (Lambert zones) and NAD83.
Convert between grid and ground distances using the combined scale factor.
Apply convergence to relate grid azimuth and geodetic azimuth.
key_takeaways: Texas uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection in five SPCS zones on NAD83.
Ground distance = grid distance / combined scale factor (CSF = grid scale factor * elevation factor).
Convergence is the angle between grid north and geodetic north; grid azimuth = geodetic azimuth - convergence.

content.html:
State plane coordinates in Texas

The State Plane Coordinate System (SPCS) lets surveyors work in plane rectangular coordinates while staying tied to the national geodetic datum (NAD83). Texas is divided into five zones — North, North Central, Central, South Central, and South — each using the Lambert Conformal Conic projection, which is well suited to Texas's predominantly east-west extent. Coordinates are published in U.S. survey feet (and meters) as northings and eastings.

Because a curved Earth is being mapped onto a flat grid, grid distances and directions differ slightly from their ground (geodetic) counterparts. Advanced surveyors must convert deliberately and document which system every value is in.

Grid vs. ground: the combined scale factor

Two effects separate grid distance from ground distance. The grid scale factor accounts for the projection (the grid is slightly too short or too long depending on position relative to the standard parallels), and the elevation (sea-level) factor reduces a ground distance to the ellipsoid. Their product is the combined scale factor (CSF). To go from grid to ground you divide; to go from ground to grid you multiply.

Convergence

Grid north (the direction of the central meridian's projection) generally does not coincide with geodetic (true) north except on the central meridian. The angle between them is the convergence (mapping angle), gamma. To relate directions: grid azimuth = geodetic azimuth − convergence. East of the central meridian convergence is positive (grid north lies west of true north); west of it, negative. Ignoring convergence corrupts any azimuth carried between grid and geodetic systems.

content.text: Texas differs: state plane coordinates are mandatory or expected on many public projects, and Texas surveyors must clearly label whether reported bearings/distances are grid or surface (ground). A surface-adjusted, low-distortion projection or a stated CSF is common on TxDOT work; never combine grid coordinates with ground distances without converting.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Texas uses Lambert Conformal Conic in five SPCS zones on NAD83.
Ground = grid / CSF; CSF = grid scale factor * elevation factor.
Convergence ties grid and geodetic azimuths: grid Az = geodetic Az - gamma.

flashcards.definition: A plane-coordinate system on NAD83 using the Lambert Conformal Conic projection in five Texas zones (North, North Central, Central, South Central, South).
The product of the grid scale factor and the elevation factor; divide a grid distance by CSF to get ground distance, multiply to go the other way.
The angle between grid north and geodetic north; grid azimuth = geodetic azimuth - convergence. Positive east of the central meridian.

lessons.title: Week 13: Introduction to Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
learning_objectives: Define photogrammetry and the geometry of a vertical aerial photograph.
Compute photo scale and relate flying height, focal length, and ground coverage.
Contrast photogrammetry with active remote sensing (LiDAR, radar) and their products.
key_takeaways: Photogrammetry derives measurements and 3D models from overlapping photographs.
Photo scale = f/(H - h); relief and tilt displace image points from their true map position.
Remote sensing (passive imagery and active LiDAR/radar) extends mapping over large areas.

content.html:
Measuring from images

Photogrammetry is the science of obtaining reliable measurements and 3D models from photographs. In aerial mapping, an aircraft flies parallel strips, taking vertical photographs with substantial forward overlap (typically about 60%) and side lap (about 30%) so that every ground point appears on multiple images. Overlapping pairs are viewed stereoscopically to perceive and measure relief, and ground control points tie the model to real-world coordinates through aerotriangulation.

content.label: Photo scale (f = focal length, H = flying height above datum, h = ground elevation)

content.html:
Relief and tilt displacement

A vertical photograph is a perspective projection, not a map. Two distortions matter: relief displacement shifts the image of an elevated point radially outward from the principal point (taller or higher points lean outward), and tilt displacement arises when the camera axis is not perfectly vertical. Orthorectification removes these to produce a true-scale orthophoto. Understanding relief displacement is also how heights of objects can be measured from a single photo.

Remote sensing beyond photographs

Remote sensing broadens data collection to passive multispectral imagery (for land cover and vegetation indices) and active sensors. LiDAR emits laser pulses and times their return to produce dense, accurate 3D point clouds — now a primary source for DTMs and corridor mapping. Radar (InSAR) penetrates clouds and can measure ground deformation. Each product (orthophoto, DSM/DTM, point cloud, classified imagery) has appropriate and inappropriate surveying uses; a surveyor must understand the accuracy and the control behind any derived product before relying on it.

content.text: Photogrammetric and LiDAR products are only as good as their ground control and accuracy specification. A surveyor of record must verify that derived mapping meets the project's accuracy standard before certifying or designing from it.

content.alt: Vertical aerial photo geometry with focal length, flying height, overlap, and relief displacement
content.caption: Figure: Vertical photo geometry, photo scale, and relief displacement.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Photogrammetry extracts measurements and 3D models from overlapping photos.
Photo scale S = f/(H - h); relief and tilt displace image points.
Remote sensing adds passive imagery and active LiDAR/radar; verify control and accuracy.

flashcards.definition: The ratio S = f/(H - h) of focal length to the flying height above the ground point; ground distance = photo distance / S.
The radial outward shift of an elevated point's image from the principal point on a vertical photo; used to measure object heights and removed by orthorectification.
An active remote-sensing method that times laser-pulse returns to build dense 3D point clouds, widely used for DTMs and corridor mapping.

lessons.title: Week 14: Survey Project Management and Professional Practice
learning_objectives: Outline survey project management: scope, schedule, budget, and QA/QC.
Describe accuracy specifications, deliverables, and standards governing professional work.
Explain the responsibilities of the surveyor of record and TBPELS rules in Texas.
key_takeaways: A survey project is managed for scope, schedule, budget, safety, and quality with documented QA/QC.
Deliverables are governed by accuracy standards and by the client contract and applicable specifications.
In Texas the RPLS and the surveyor of record bear legal responsibility under TBPELS rules.

content.html:
Managing a survey project

Technical skill is only half of professional practice; the other half is project management. A well-run survey defines a clear scope of work, a realistic schedule, and a budget, and it plans field safety and quality control from the outset. The project manager allocates crews and equipment, tracks progress against milestones, manages subconsultants (aerial, geotechnical), and maintains client communication. Scope creep, undocumented assumptions, and inadequate control are the usual causes of overruns and disputes.

rows: What deliverables and to what standard?
When must each milestone be met?
What labor, equipment, and subs are funded?
How is accuracy verified and documented?

content.html:
Standards, specifications, and deliverables

Survey deliverables must meet a stated accuracy standard and the requirements of the contract and any governing specification. Professional and federal standards (e.g., ALTA/NSPS Land Title Surveys, NGS/FGDC positional-accuracy standards, and agency specs like TxDOT survey manuals) define classification, allowable error, and reporting. Every deliverable should document the datum, basis of bearing, control used, equipment, methods, and the achieved accuracy, so another professional can evaluate and rely on the work.

content.text: Texas differs: professional land surveying in Texas is regulated by the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS). A Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) must take responsible charge of and seal surveys; the surveyor of record is legally accountable for the work, and TBPELS rules govern boundary procedures, seals, and conduct.

content.html:
Responsible charge and professional conduct

The surveyor of record exercises responsible charge — direct control and personal professional judgment — over the survey, even when crews and technicians perform field and office tasks. This means reviewing the work, verifying that it meets the standard, and sealing the deliverable. Ethics, competence within one's qualifications, and honest reporting of accuracy are not optional add-ons; they are the foundation of the public trust the license represents.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Manage scope, schedule, budget, safety, and QA/QC from the start.
Deliverables must meet stated accuracy standards and document datum/basis/methods.
In Texas the RPLS in responsible charge is legally accountable under TBPELS rules.

flashcards.definition: The direct control and personal professional judgment a licensed surveyor exercises over a survey, required to seal the work.
The licensed professional (in Texas, an RPLS) legally accountable for a survey, who takes responsible charge and seals the deliverable.
The stated standard (e.g., ALTA/NSPS, NGS/FGDC, agency spec) defining allowable error, classification, and reporting for a survey deliverable.

lessons.title: Week 15: Comprehensive Review and SIT/RPLS Exam Preparation
learning_objectives: Synthesize the full course: COGO, curves, earthwork, control, SPCS, and management.
Map course topics to SIT/RPLS exam domains and practice integrated problems.
Build an exam strategy: units, checks, and time management.
key_takeaways: The SIT (FS) and RPLS (PS) exams test the integrated competencies this course develops.
Strong candidates manage units rigorously, keep full precision, and verify with independent checks.
Integrated problems combine curves, coordinates, areas, and grid-ground reductions.

content.html:
Pulling it together

The final review week consolidates SRVY 2341 into a coherent picture and connects it to the licensure path. In Texas the route to licensure runs through the NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam — the basis of the Surveyor-in-Training (SIT) credential — followed by the Principles and Practice of Surveying (PS) exam and Texas-specific requirements for the RPLS. The competencies you built this term — coordinate geometry, horizontal and vertical curves, earthwork, topographic methods, state plane reductions, photogrammetry literacy, and professional practice — map directly onto these exams' domains.

rows: Core computations on FS and PS

content.html:
Integrated problem solving

Exam problems rarely test one idea in isolation. A typical integrated problem might give grid coordinates for a PI, ask you to reduce a measured ground distance with a combined scale factor, design a curve to fit, compute the PC/PT stations, and then find an area or volume. The skill is recognizing which tool each step needs and carrying consistent units and full precision through the chain. Practice with multi-step problems, not just single-formula drills.

content.text: Exam-day discipline: (1) write down what is given and what is asked, (2) keep one consistent unit system, (3) carry full calculator precision, (4) sanity-check magnitude, and (5) use independent relationships (deflection = I/2; PT = PC + L; area sign) as built-in checks.

content.html:
Targeted review

Use the midterm and quizzes to find weak spots and drill them. Common high-value review items: converting bearings to azimuths cleanly, the half-angle curve formulas, vertical-curve units (percent and stations), grid-ground direction (which way to divide vs. multiply), and the meaning of convergence. A short, focused review of these recurring traps yields more points than re-reading everything.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: This course maps onto the FS/SIT and PS/RPLS exam domains.
Integrated problems chain COGO, curves, areas, and grid-ground reductions.
Win points with unit discipline, full precision, and independent checks.

flashcards.definition: The credential earned by passing the NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam; the first step toward Texas RPLS licensure.
An exam problem that chains several competencies (e.g., grid-ground reduction, curve design, stationing, area) requiring consistent units and full precision.
Independent relationships used to verify answers: total deflection = I/2, PT = PC + L, and the sign/magnitude of an area by coordinates.

lessons.title: Week 16: Final Exam
learning_objectives: Demonstrate comprehensive mastery across all course topics on the final exam.
Solve integrated multi-step problems under time constraints.
Apply professional judgment to units, accuracy, and reporting.
key_takeaways: The final is comprehensive: COGO, all curves, earthwork, topo, SPCS, photogrammetry, and practice.
Plan time, read each problem fully, and identify the tool before computing.
Report answers in requested units with appropriate significant figures and independent checks.

content.html:
Final exam — comprehensive scope

The Week 16 final covers the entire course. Expect questions spanning coordinate geometry; simple, compound, reverse, and vertical curves; route surveying; earthwork volumes; construction staking; topographic methods and contours; state plane coordinates and grid-ground reduction; introductory photogrammetry and remote sensing; and survey project management and professional practice. Both conceptual and computational questions appear, with several integrated multi-step problems.

content.text: Final-exam strategy: budget time per point, attempt every problem, show enough work that a partially correct method earns partial credit, and reserve a few minutes to apply unit and magnitude checks before submitting.

content.html:
After the course

The competencies validated here carry directly into professional field practice in construction and route surveying and into the FS/SIT examination. Keep your problem-solving discipline — consistent units, full precision, and independent checks — as a permanent professional habit. These are the same checks that catch costly errors on real projects.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The final is comprehensive across all 15 prior weeks.
Identify the right tool before computing; manage time across problems.
Report in requested units with significant figures and built-in checks.

flashcards.definition: All course domains: COGO, horizontal/vertical curves, route surveying, earthwork, construction staking, topo, SPCS, photogrammetry, and professional practice.
Show enough method that a correct approach earns credit even if an arithmetic slip changes the final number; attempt every problem.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the inverse computation between two coordinate points, which helper pair correctly gives the distance and azimuth?
options: sin for distance, cos for azimuth
hypot for distance, atan2 for azimuth
tan for distance, asin for azimuth
sqrt for azimuth, atan for distance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: hypot for distance, atan2 for azimuth
quiz_questions.explanation: Distance is the hypotenuse of the latitude and departure (hypot of dN and dE); azimuth is atan2(dE, dN). atan2 resolves the correct quadrant automatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which two quantities completely define a simple horizontal curve?
quiz_questions.explanation: Given the radius R and the intersection (central) angle I, every other element (T, L, E, M, LC) follows by trigonometry on I/2.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the arc definition, a 2-degree curve has approximately what radius?
If the PI is at station 24+00, T = 150 ft, and L = 280 ft, what is the PT station?
quiz_questions.explanation: PC = PI - T = 2400 - 150 = 2250 (22+50). PT = PC + L = 2250 + 280 = 2530, i.e., 25+30. Using PI + T would be the classic blunder.

quiz_questions.question_text: The point of tangency (PT) station is found by adding the tangent distance T to the PI station.
quiz_questions.explanation: PT = PC + L (arc length). Adding T to the PI overstates the position because the arc is shorter than running out to the PI and back.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two circular arcs curving in the same direction with different radii, joined on a common tangent, form a:
quiz_questions.explanation: Same direction + different radii + common tangent at a PCC defines a compound curve. Opposite directions would be a reverse curve.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why are reverse curves without an intervening tangent or spiral discouraged on high-speed highways?
options: Superelevation cannot reverse instantaneously at the PRC
Their radius is always too large
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Superelevation cannot reverse instantaneously at the PRC
quiz_questions.explanation: At the PRC the cross-slope (banking) must change sense; without a transition length the superelevation cannot be reversed safely, so a tangent or spiral is provided.

quiz_questions.question_text: A vertical curve where the incoming grade is +3% and the outgoing grade is -2% is a:
quiz_questions.explanation: The grade decreases (g1 > g2: +3 to -2), forming a hill, which is a crest curve. A sag has g1 < g2.

quiz_questions.question_text: A vertical curve has g1 = +2.0% and r = +0.5% per station. At how many stations from the BVC is the low (turning) point? Report stations to 1 decimal.
quiz_questions.explanation: x_t = -g1/r = -(2.0)/(0.5)... sign convention: the magnitude is |g1/r| = 2.0/0.5 = 4.0 stations. With g1 positive and r positive this sag's grade reaches zero 4.0 stations from the BVC.

quiz_questions.question_text: A crest vertical curve must satisfy a minimum K of 61 with an algebraic grade change A = 5.0%. What minimum curve length L (ft) is required? Report to the nearest foot.
A station equation such as '45+20 back = 45+00 ahead' is used to:
options: Reconcile a change in alignment length so stationing stays continuous
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Reconcile a change in alignment length so stationing stays continuous
quiz_questions.explanation: When a revised alignment changes length, a station equation ties back-stationing to ahead-stationing so downstream stations need not be renumbered.

quiz_questions.question_text: Horizontal and vertical alignments should be designed independently of each other.
quiz_questions.explanation: A route is a 3D object; the two alignments are coordinated so curves, sight distance, drainage, and superelevation work together.

quiz_questions.question_text: The average-end-area volume between two cross-sections is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Average end area multiplies the mean of the two end areas by the distance between sections. The d/6(A1+4Am+A2) form is the more accurate prismoidal formula.

quiz_questions.question_text: A slope stake marks the location of the:
options: Catch (daylight) point where the side slope meets existing ground
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Catch (daylight) point where the side slope meets existing ground
quiz_questions.explanation: The slope stake locates the catch/daylight point; its offset depends on slope ratio, depth, and ground slope.

quiz_questions.question_text: On level ground, a cut section has template half-width w = 20 ft, side slope 2:1 (s = 2), and cut depth h = 6 ft. What is the catch-point offset from centerline (ft)?
Which statement about contour lines is correct?
options: Closely spaced contours indicate steep ground
Contour interval must increase along a single map
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Closely spaced contours indicate steep ground
quiz_questions.explanation: Contours never cross (except at a vertical cliff) and a constant interval is used on a map; closely spaced contours mean a steep slope.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas State Plane zones use which map projection?
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas's five SPCS zones use the Lambert Conformal Conic projection, suited to the state's east-west extent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A grid distance is 2500.00 ft and the combined scale factor is 0.99988. What is the ground distance (ft)? Report to 2 decimals.
On a vertical aerial photograph, photo scale equals:
quiz_questions.explanation: Photo scale S = f/(H - h), the ratio of focal length to flying height above the ground point.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas, an RPLS in responsible charge is legally accountable for a survey and must seal the work under TBPELS rules.
quiz_questions.explanation: The Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS) requires a Registered Professional Land Surveyor in responsible charge to seal the survey, and that surveyor of record is legally accountable.

quiz_questions.question_text: An exam problem gives grid coordinates for a PI, a measured ground tangent distance, and a combined scale factor, then asks for the PC station. In what order are the tools applied?
options: Find PT first, then reduce grid to ground
Reduce ground distance to grid, then compute curve elements and PC = PI - T
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Reduce ground distance to grid, then compute curve elements and PC = PI - T
quiz_questions.explanation: Work in one consistent system: convert distances with the CSF, compute T from R and I, then PC = PI - T. Recognizing the tool for each step is the integrated-problem skill.

problem_templates.name: Latitude and departure of a course
problem_templates.question_template: A line has length {{dist:f2}} ft on an azimuth of {{az:f1}} deg from north. Compute the LATITUDE (north-south component, N positive) of the line in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Latitude = L cos(Az) = {{dist:f2}} x cos({{az:f1}} deg) = {{_answer}} ft. (Its departure, L sin(Az), would be {{dep:f2}} ft east.)

problem_templates.name: Traverse misclosure by hypot of latitude and departure
problem_templates.question_template: After summing a traverse, the closure in latitudes is {{clat:f3}} ft and the closure in departures is {{cdep:f3}} ft. Compute the LINEAR MISCLOSURE (the length of the closure vector) in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Linear misclosure = sqrt(dLat^2 + dDep^2) = hypot({{clat:f3}}, {{cdep:f3}}) = {{_answer}} ft. This is divided by the traverse perimeter to express the relative precision (1:n).

problem_templates.name: Grid-to-ground distance via combined scale factor
problem_templates.question_template: A grid distance is {{grid:f2}} ft. The grid scale factor is {{gsf:f5}} and the elevation factor is {{ef:f5}}. Compute the GROUND distance in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: CSF = {{gsf:f5}} x {{ef:f5}} = {{csf:f5}}. Ground = grid / CSF = {{grid:f2}} / {{csf:f5}} = {{_answer}} ft. (Multiply to go from ground back to grid.)

problem_templates.name: Tangent distance of a simple horizontal curve
problem_templates.question_template: A simple horizontal curve has radius R = {{R:f1}} ft and intersection angle I = {{I:f1}} deg. Compute the TANGENT distance T in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: T = R tan(I/2) = {{R:f1}} x tan({{I:f1}}/2) = {{_answer}} ft. (The arc length L = R*I(rad) = {{L:f2}} ft.)

problem_templates.name: Curve length of a simple horizontal curve
problem_templates.question_template: A simple horizontal curve has radius R = {{R:f1}} ft and intersection angle I = {{I:f1}} deg. Compute the ARC LENGTH L in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: L = R * I(radians) = {{R:f1}} x toRad({{I:f1}}) = {{_answer}} ft. (The tangent T = R tan(I/2) = {{T:f2}} ft.)

problem_templates.name: Vertical curve elevation at an offset station
problem_templates.question_template: An equal-tangent vertical curve has BVC elevation {{ybvc:f2}} ft, incoming grade g1 = {{g1:f2}} percent, outgoing grade g2 = {{g2:f2}} percent, and total length L = {{Lst:f1}} stations. Compute the curve elevation (ft) at x = {{x:f1}} stations from the BVC.
problem_templates.explanation_template: With grades in percent and x in stations, Y = Y_BVC + g1*x + (r/2)x^2 where r = (g2-g1)/L = {{r:f4}}. Substituting gives {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Area of a parcel by coordinates (triangle)
problem_templates.question_template: A triangular parcel has corners A({{na:f1}}, {{ea:f1}}), B({{nb:f1}}, {{eb:f1}}), and C({{nc:f1}}, {{ec:f1}}) as (Northing, Easting) in feet. Compute the AREA in square feet by the coordinate method.

solution_steps_template.title: Cross-product sum
solution_steps_template.formula: Sum = E_A(N_B - N_C) + E_B(N_C - N_A) + E_C(N_A - N_B)

problem_templates.explanation_template: The coordinate (shoelace) method gives A = |E_A(N_B-N_C) + E_B(N_C-N_A) + E_C(N_A-N_B)| / 2 = |{{cross:f1}}| / 2 = {{_answer}} sq ft. The sign of the sum reflects only vertex order.
